narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your law school journey, including your undergraduate studies and LSAT preparation. The admissions model we use evaluates applicants based on historical data from students who took the bar exam, predicting the likelihood of passing on the first try to ensure we select those most likely to succeed. For you, the model estimated a 53.1% probability of failing the bar exam, which tipped the scales just enough to result in the rejection of your application—it's a close call, but the threshold requires stronger indicators of success. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 4th, which is below the typical 5.5 average and signaled to the model a dip in performance compared to most peers. Your family income bracket, while upper-middle and a positive in many ways, also nudged the outlook slightly toward higher risk in this model's eyes relative to the dataset. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 30 worked strongly in your favor, pushing back against failure since it's a key strength even if below the 36.7 average. Your Year 1 decile of 5 and choice to pursue full-time studies—common for 93% of students—provided additional positive influences, helping offset some concerns. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.0 was right around the average of 3.21, so it played a neutral role overall. Compared to typical students who pass, your profile shows competitive elements like full-time commitment but stands out with that lower Year 3 decile and LSAT that didn't quite match the stronger performers. This rejection doesn't define your potential; it's just one data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and reapplying next cycle with an updated profile that addresses these key areas—we'd be happy to review it again. Keep pushing; many successful lawyers faced early hurdles like this.
